STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE
Title: Deviation Handling and Investigation
Document Number: SOP-QA-042
Version: 3.2
Effective Date: 2025-01-15
Department: Quality Assurance
Author: J. Martinez
Reviewed By: (Pending)
Approved By: (Pending)

1. PURPOSE

This procedure establishes the requirements for identifying, documenting, investigating, and resolving deviations from established procedures, specifications, or regulatory requirements at our manufacturing facility.

2. (no section header — scope content begins here)

This SOP applies to all manufacturing, laboratory, and quality operations where a deviation from an approved procedure, specification, or regulatory requirement has occurred or is suspected. Applicable to all personnel in Production, QC, and QA departments.

3. DEFINITIONS

3.1 Deviation: Any departure from an approved instruction, procedure, specification, or established standard.
3.2 Planned Deviation: A pre-approved departure from a procedure before it occurs.
3.3 Unplanned Deviation: A departure from a procedure that is identified after it occurs.
3.4 CAPA: Corrective Action and Preventive Action.
3.5 Root Cause: The fundamental reason for the occurrence of a problem.

4. RESPONSIBILITIES

4.1 Manufacturing Personnel: Responsible for identifying and immediately reporting deviations to the supervisor.
4.2 Supervisor: Responsible for initiating deviation reports and preliminary assessment.
4.3 Quality Assurance: Responsible for deviation classification, investigation oversight, and approval.
4.4 Quality Control: Responsible for laboratory testing as applicable to the deviation.

5. PROCEDURE

5.1 Deviation Identification and Reporting

5.1.1 Any personnel who identifies a deviation shall report it to their supervisor immediately upon discovery.
5.1.2 The supervisor shall initiate a Deviation Report (Form QA-042-F01) within 24 hours of identification.
5.1.3 The Deviation Report shall include: date of occurrence, description of the deviation, affected batch numbers, and preliminary assessment.

5.2 Deviation Classification

5.2.1 Quality Assurance shall classify the deviation as needed within 2 business days:
   - Critical: Potential impact on product quality, patient safety, or regulatory compliance
   - Major: Potential impact on product quality but unlikely to affect patient safety
   - Minor: No direct impact on product quality

5.2.2 Classification shall be based on risk assessment if applicable.

5.3 Investigation

5.3.1 A thorough investigation shall be conducted for all Critical and Major deviations.
5.3.2 The investigation shall identify the root cause using appropriate tools as appropriate.
5.3.3 Investigation shall be completed within 30 calendar days for Critical deviations and 45 days for Major deviations.
5.3.4 Minor deviations may be documented with a brief explanation.

5.4 Impact Assessment

5.4.1 QA shall assess the impact of the deviation on product quality and patient safety.
5.4.2 Impact assessment shall consider: affected batches, product disposition, and regulatory reporting requirements.
5.4.3 Products associated with Critical deviations shall be placed on hold pending disposition decision.

5.5 Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA)

5.5.1 CAPAs shall be initiated for all Critical and Major deviations.
5.5.2 The CAPA shall address the root cause identified during investigation.
5.5.3 CAPA effectiveness shall be verified on an ongoing basis following implementation.

5.6 Closure

5.6.1 Deviations may be closed when all required documentation is complete and approved by QA.
5.6.2 Closed deviation records shall be retained for the period required by regulation.

6. DOCUMENTATION

6.1 Deviation Report Form: QA-042-F01
6.2 CAPA Request Form: QA-CAPA-001-F01
6.3 Deviation Log maintained by Quality Assurance Department

7. REGULATORY REQUIREMENTS

This SOP is designed to comply with 21 CFR 211.100, 21 CFR 211.192, and ICH Q10 requirements for deviation management and CAPA systems.

Revision History: See Document Control System for revision history.
